evidence_quotes.quote: video games and how some of them require an internet connection in some cases to function.

paragraphs.text: I think this is an amazing idea, but might I suggest including other digital things? A big example right now, in the modern landscape, has to do with the topic of video games and how some of them require an internet connection in some cases to function. Take the popular hit game right now, Fortnite, and how people who've spent money on it will most likely lose all they've earned when the servers close for good as there's no built in measures to ensure the player can enjoy the game offline. It might take a while for big games like Fortnite to go down, but there are examples already out there of video game publishers/developers ending support for games that required an internet connection, and even in some cases required you to BUY the game, which unlike Fortnite is a mostly free to play game, with optional monetization through cosmetics. Take Overwatch, which was shut down by Blizzard to make way for their "sequel" which was more or less just the same game but with les features and leaving the people who bought the original back then 40 euros short from their wallets considering Overwatch 2, the sequel, is free to play. Ubisoft also shut down the servers for a popular racing game called The Crew not too long ago, which despite having a "single player" experience, customers are unable to access it since it got taken down from store fronts, and even if they own the game physically, when inserted into a console they will be prompted with an online connection to the servers that won't be possible due to the servers shutting down, so even the features that don't require an internet connection are inaccessible, all because the company chose to restrict them behind an active online wall. I think video game would greatly benefit from being protected as well as these companies can be heavily predatory.
